content.problemStatement:
The  **`break`**  statement is a control statement in Python that allows you to exit a loop prematurely.


When the  `break`  statement is encountered inside a loop,  the loop is immediately terminated
The program execution continues with the next statement after the loop
Example:

`fruits = ["apple", "banana", "orange", "grape"]

for item in fruits:
    if item == "orange":
        break
    print(item)
`Output:

`apple
banana
`In this example, the FOR loop iterates over each fruit in the  `fruits`  list.

Inside the loop,  there is an `if` statement that checks if the current fruit is equal to "orange".

When the condition becomes true, the  `break`  statement is executed, and the loop is immediately terminated.

Explanation for the above example:

To summarise
The loop will start with "apple", check whether it is equal to "orange", as it is not equal, the loop will continue.
Then the loop will run for "banana", again it is not equal to "orange", the loop will continue.
Then the loop will run for "orange" and the condition becomes `True`.

In this case `break` is executed and the loop is exited.
Because the loop exited as soon as `break` was found, the Print(item) will not run for "orange".

**


Your Task!
Write a program which does the following



You are given a list consisting of 12 integers - the number of days in each month in a year
You need to iterate through the  `loop` and print the number of days
Exit the  `loop`  if you find any integer which is less than 30, output this integer before breaking the loop
constraints: Each integer is between 1 and 31 inclusive
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains exactly 12 space‑separated integers representing the number of days in the months of a year.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the integers one per line in the order they appear until an integer strictly less than 30 is encountered. Print that integer as well and then stop processing the current test case. Continue with the next test case (if any).

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first month has 31 days and is printed. The second month has 28 days, which is less than 30, so it is printed and the loop stops.
All months have 31 days, none are less than 30, so all twelve numbers are printed.
The very first month has 28 days (<30). It is printed and the loop terminates immediately.

solution.approach: For each test case read the 12 integers into an array (or process them on the fly). Iterate from the first month to the last, printing the current value. After printing, check if the value is strictly less than 30; if so, break out of the loop for this test case. Continue with the next test case. The algorithm is a straightforward linear scan; no additional data structures are required.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 12) = O(T) because we examine at most 12 numbers per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input storage (the array of 12 integers).
